# ARTICLE 1

**Title:** how I built a website audit tool in a weekend with no backend

**Tags:** `webdev` `javascript` `opensource` `tutorial`

**Canonical URL:** (set on publish)

**Cover image alt:** browser devtools open next to sitescore-app.surge.sh showing a 91/100 score with audit breakdown

**Live tool:** https://sitescore-app.surge.sh

---

i built a site audit tool that competes with $99/month products. it took a weekend. it has no backend, no database, no server bills, and no API keys the user has to supply.

here's the full technical breakdown of how it works.

## the problem with existing free tools

Google PageSpeed Insights is the obvious free option. it's good for Core Web Vitals and performance scores. it's bad for structured, scannable reporting.

GTmetrix gives you better UI but charges $21-$220/month depending on monitoring volume. Ahrefs Site Audit gives you complete crawling but you're paying $99/month for the whole suite.

the gap i noticed: nobody had a free tool that combined performance scoring with basic on-page SEO checks and presented it as a single health score with specific fix recommendations. so i built it.

## the architecture

three data sources, all free, all accessible from client-side JavaScript:

1. Google PageSpeed Insights API (no auth required for basic queries)
2. allorigins.win CORS proxy (for fetching and parsing HTML)
3. browser-native DOMParser (for on-page analysis)

no server. the user's browser does all the work.

## source 1: PageSpeed Insights API

this is the backbone. it returns Lighthouse scores for Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, and SEO, plus Core Web Vitals including LCP, CLS, and TTFB.

the API endpoint requires no authentication for basic usage:

```javascript
async function fetchPageSpeedData(url) {
  const endpoint = 'https://www.googleapis.com/pagespeedonline/v5/runPagespeed';
  const params = new URLSearchParams({
    url: url,
    strategy: 'mobile',
    category: ['performance', 'accessibility', 'best-practices', 'seo']
  });

  const response = await fetch(`${endpoint}?${params}`);
  if (!response.ok) throw new Error(`PageSpeed API returned ${response.status}`);
  return response.json();
}

function extractScores(data) {
  const cats = data.lighthouseResult.categories;
  const audits = data.lighthouseResult.audits;

  return {
    performance: Math.round(cats.performance.score * 100),
    accessibility: Math.round(cats.accessibility.score * 100),
    bestPractices: Math.round(cats['best-practices'].score * 100),
    seo: Math.round(cats.seo.score * 100),
    lcp: audits['largest-contentful-paint'].displayValue,
    cls: audits['cumulative-layout-shift'].displayValue,
    ttfb: audits['server-response-time'].displayValue,
    fcp: audits['first-contentful-paint'].displayValue,
    speedIndex: audits['speed-index'].displayValue,
    tbt: audits['total-blocking-time'].displayValue
  };
}
```

the free tier allows 25,000 requests per day. for a tool with low-to-moderate usage, this is more than enough. if you hit the limit, add a free API key from the Google Cloud Console (still free, just authenticated).

## source 2: HTML fetching via CORS proxy

browsers block cross-origin fetches by default. if you try to `fetch("https://somesite.com")` from your JavaScript, you'll get a CORS error because that site's server doesn't have the right headers.

two solutions:

**option A: allorigins.win**, a free, open-source CORS proxy. you send it a URL, it fetches the page and returns the HTML to you with the right CORS headers.

**option B: a textarea fallback**, let the user paste their site's HTML directly. no proxy needed, no CORS issue, works 100% of the time.

i use both. allorigins.win for the default flow, textarea fallback if the proxy is slow or rate-limited.

```javascript
async function fetchHTML(url) {
  try {
    const proxyUrl = `https://api.allorigins.win/get?url=${encodeURIComponent(url)}`;
    const response = await fetch(proxyUrl, { signal: AbortSignal.timeout(8000) });
    const data = await response.json();
    if (!data.contents) throw new Error('Empty response from proxy');
    return data.contents;
  } catch (err) {
    // surface the error to the UI, prompt manual paste fallback
    throw new Error(`Could not fetch ${url}: ${err.message}`);
  }
}
```

the `AbortSignal.timeout(8000)` is important. allorigins.win occasionally hangs. without the timeout, the user sits on a spinner indefinitely. 8 seconds is generous enough for slow sites and strict enough to fail fast.

## source 3: on-page analysis with DOMParser

once you have the raw HTML string, DOMParser handles the rest. no external dependency, no server call, zero cost:

```javascript
function analyzeHTML(htmlString) {
  const parser = new DOMParser();
  const doc = parser.parseFromString(htmlString, 'text/html');

  const title = doc.title;
  const metaDesc = doc.querySelector('meta[name="description"]');
  const canonical = doc.querySelector('link[rel="canonical"]');
  const h1s = doc.querySelectorAll('h1');
  const images = doc.querySelectorAll('img');
  const imagesWithoutAlt = doc.querySelectorAll('img:not([alt]), img[alt=""]');
  const internalLinks = doc.querySelectorAll('a[href^="/"], a[href^="./"]');
  const externalLinks = doc.querySelectorAll('a[href^="http"]');

  // structured data check
  const jsonLd = doc.querySelectorAll('script[type="application/ld+json"]');
  const ogTags = doc.querySelectorAll('meta[property^="og:"]');
  const twitterCards = doc.querySelectorAll('meta[name^="twitter:"]');

  // viewport meta check (critical for mobile-first indexing)
  const viewport = doc.querySelector('meta[name="viewport"]');

  return {
    title: {
      text: title,
      length: title.length,
      pass: title.length >= 30 && title.length <= 60
    },
    metaDescription: {
      exists: !!metaDesc,
      length: metaDesc?.getAttribute('content')?.length || 0,
      pass: metaDesc && metaDesc.getAttribute('content')?.length >= 120 && metaDesc.getAttribute('content')?.length <= 160
    },
    h1: {
      count: h1s.length,
      pass: h1s.length === 1
    },
    images: {
      total: images.length,
      missingAlt: imagesWithoutAlt.length,
      pass: imagesWithoutAlt.length === 0
    },
    links: {
      internal: internalLinks.length,
      external: externalLinks.length
    },
    canonical: {
      exists: !!canonical,
      href: canonical?.getAttribute('href') || null,
      pass: !!canonical
    },
    structuredData: {
      hasJsonLd: jsonLd.length > 0,
      schemas: jsonLd.length,
      pass: jsonLd.length > 0
    },
    socialMeta: {
      ogCount: ogTags.length,
      twitterCardCount: twitterCards.length,
      pass: ogTags.length >= 4
    },
    viewport: {
      exists: !!viewport,
      content: viewport?.getAttribute('content') || null,
      pass: viewport?.getAttribute('content')?.includes('width=device-width')
    }
  };
}
```

that's 14 distinct checks with zero server cost. the `pass` fields let you show a simple green/red indicator in the UI without any scoring logic in the display layer.

## the scoring algorithm

i wanted a single score out of 100. here's how i weight it:

```javascript
function calculateOverallScore(pageSpeedScores, onPageChecks) {
  // pageSpeed component (60% of total score)
  const pageSpeedComponent = (
    pageSpeedScores.performance * 0.35 +
    pageSpeedScores.seo * 0.25 +
    pageSpeedScores.accessibility * 0.20 +
    pageSpeedScores.bestPractices * 0.20
  ) * 0.60;

  // on-page component (40% of total score)
  const checks = [
    onPageChecks.title.pass,
    onPageChecks.metaDescription.pass,
    onPageChecks.h1.pass,
    onPageChecks.images.pass,
    onPageChecks.canonical.pass,
    onPageChecks.structuredData.pass,
    onPageChecks.socialMeta.pass,
    onPageChecks.viewport.pass
  ];
  const passedChecks = checks.filter(Boolean).length;
  const onPageComponent = (passedChecks / checks.length) * 100 * 0.40;

  return Math.round(pageSpeedComponent + onPageComponent);
}
```

the 60/40 split between PageSpeed and on-page checks is a judgment call. performance gets more weight because it directly affects both user experience and Google's ranking signals. adjust these weights for your audience. an agency tool auditing enterprise sites might flip the ratio.

## the UI pattern: parallel fetches, single loading state

the PageSpeed API call and the HTML fetch take different amounts of time. running them in parallel cuts total wait time from ~12 seconds to ~8 seconds:

```javascript
async function runFullAudit(url) {
  showLoadingState();

  try {
    const [pageSpeedData, htmlContent] = await Promise.allSettled([
      fetchPageSpeedData(url),
      fetchHTML(url)
    ]);

    const scores = pageSpeedData.status === 'fulfilled'
      ? extractScores(pageSpeedData.value)
      : null;

    const onPage = htmlContent.status === 'fulfilled'
      ? analyzeHTML(htmlContent.value)
      : null;

    renderResults(url, scores, onPage);
  } catch (err) {
    showError(err.message);
  }
}
```

`Promise.allSettled` matters here over `Promise.all`. if the CORS proxy fails (it does occasionally), `Promise.all` would reject everything. `Promise.allSettled` lets you show the PageSpeed data even when the HTML fetch fails. partial results are better than a full error screen.

## deployment

```bash
surge . sitescore-app.surge.sh
```

28 seconds. $0 hosting.

the tool is live at https://sitescore-app.surge.sh. no account required. no email required. just paste a URL and hit run.

## what it actually catches that PageSpeed misses

PageSpeed tells you scores. it does not check:

- whether you have a canonical tag (PageSpeed doesn't parse for this)
- whether your H1 count is right (1 is correct, 0 or 3+ are problems)
- whether images are missing alt text (PageSpeed flags this in accessibility but doesn't give you a count)
- whether your Open Graph tags are present (affects social sharing appearance)
- whether you have structured data (affects rich results eligibility)

these are not exotic checks. every page on every site should pass all of them. most don't.

## the honest tradeoffs

what this tool does not do:

- no full-site crawl. one URL at a time.
- no historical tracking. point-in-time only.
- allorigins.win has rate limits. if it goes down, the HTML analysis degrades to "unavailable" and only PageSpeed results show.
- no backlink analysis. that requires a paid data source (Ahrefs, Majestic, etc.) and there's no free equivalent that's reliable.

for "is this page healthy," it's accurate. for "why am I ranking behind my competitor," you need more.

build time for the initial version: 6 hours. total hosting cost since launch: $0.

# ARTICLE 2

**Title:** the $0 SaaS stack: 23 tools I built without paying for a single server

**Tags:** `webdev` `javascript` `saas` `indiehackers`

---

i've shipped 23 tools in the last 6 weeks. none of them run on a server i pay for. combined hosting cost: $0. here's the full list and the architectural pattern that makes it possible.

## the tools

### SEO and site analysis

23 tools. $0 server cost. all live and loading.

## the pattern: static HTML + client-side JS + surge.sh

every tool in this list follows the same architecture:

```
tool-name/
  index.html     , complete application shell
  style.css      , all styles, CSS custom properties for theming
  app.js         , all logic
  manifest.json  , PWA metadata (for installable tools)
  sw.js          , service worker (for offline-capable tools)
  icons/         , 192px and 512px icons (for PWA tools)
```

no build step. no package.json. no node_modules folder. no Webpack, no Vite, no Rollup. you open index.html in a browser and it works.

deploying is one command from inside the folder:

```bash
surge . toolname.surge.sh
```

30 seconds later it's live.

## why "no-backend SaaS" works for free tools

the obvious objection: "you can't do anything real without a server."

that's wrong for a specific category of tools.

anything that processes data the user provides (invoice generator, ROI calculator, contract builder) doesn't need a server. the user gives you the input. your JavaScript transforms it. the output stays in the browser. there is no data to store server-side because there's nothing to persist between sessions.

anything that calls a public API (site auditor using PageSpeed, prospect finder using public data) doesn't need your own server. the browser calls the API directly. the API is someone else's server, and it's free.

anything that runs on the device (streak tracker using localStorage, focus timer counting seconds) doesn't need a server. the phone is the server.

the tools where this pattern breaks:

- user accounts (you need to store credentials somewhere)
- syncing data across multiple devices (you need a central store)
- sending emails or SMS (you need a server-side process with API keys)
- processing payments (Stripe requires server-side secret key handling)
- anything that reads private third-party data (requires server-side auth)

if your tool needs any of those five things, you need a backend. if it doesn't, you probably don't.

## the InvoiceForge example: why this works in practice

InvoiceForge is the tool I use to show skeptics this pattern can handle real work.

it generates a PDF invoice in the browser. the user fills in client name, line items, tax rate, and due date. clicks "Generate PDF." gets a downloadable PDF.

no file upload. no server processing the PDF. no storage.

how it works: jsPDF is a JavaScript library that runs in the browser and generates PDFs from JavaScript objects. include it with a script tag, build the invoice layout with its API, call `doc.save("invoice.pdf")`, and the browser downloads the file directly.

```javascript
// simplified example
import { jsPDF } from "jspdf";

function generateInvoice(invoiceData) {
  const doc = new jsPDF();

  doc.setFontSize(20);
  doc.text(invoiceData.businessName, 20, 30);
  doc.setFontSize(12);
  doc.text(`Invoice #${invoiceData.invoiceNumber}`, 20, 45);
  doc.text(`Date: ${invoiceData.date}`, 20, 55);
  doc.text(`Due: ${invoiceData.dueDate}`, 20, 65);

  let y = 90;
  invoiceData.lineItems.forEach(item => {
    doc.text(item.description, 20, y);
    doc.text(`$${item.amount.toFixed(2)}`, 160, y, { align: 'right' });
    y += 10;
  });

  doc.text(`Total: $${invoiceData.total.toFixed(2)}`, 160, y + 10, { align: 'right' });
  doc.save(`invoice-${invoiceData.invoiceNumber}.pdf`);
}
```

the PDF generation happens entirely in the user's browser. your server sees nothing because your server doesn't exist.

## building the audience from free tools

the free-tool-as-audience-builder play works like this:

1. build a tool that solves a real problem for a specific audience
2. put it at a memorable URL
3. post about it where that audience lives
4. the tool gets used, shared, and linked to
5. that traffic and linkage builds domain authority and social proof
6. monetize through adjacent products: a paid version with more features, a course, a template pack, a consulting offer

the free tool is the top of the funnel. it proves you can build useful things. it gets traffic without paid ads. it positions you as someone who ships.

none of the 23 tools above have a paywall. they're all free. the goal right now is audience and proof of output, not direct revenue from the tools themselves. that comes later once there's traffic data on which tools people actually use.

## the deploy script

when you have 23 sites, you need automation. here's what I run:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# deploy-all-tools.sh

TOOLS=(
  "sitescore-app"
  "pagescorer"
  "shopmetrics-pro"
  "coldmaxx-app"
  "prospectmaxx-app"
  "cold-email-calc"
  "invoiceforge-app"
  "roi-calculator"
  "stackmaxx-app"
  "focuslock-app"
  "sleepmaxx-app"
  "prayerlock-app"
  "habitforge-app"
)

for tool in "${TOOLS[@]}"; do
  if [ -d "$tool" ] && [ -f "$tool/index.html" ]; then
    echo "deploying $tool..."
    cd "$tool"
    surge . "${tool}.surge.sh" --token "$SURGE_TOKEN" 2>&1 | tail -1
    cd ..
  else
    echo "skipping $tool, folder or index.html missing"
  fi
done

echo "done. all tools deployed."
```

run it once, everything updates. total deploy time for 13 tools (the ones in the array above): about 3 minutes.

## the actual numbers

- sites deployed: 23
- server cost per month: $0
- domain cost per month: $0 (surge.sh subdomains are free)
- average deploy time per site: 28 seconds
- total time spent on deployment infrastructure: roughly 4 hours across 6 weeks
- tools that have had traffic: 19 of 23 (checked via Plausible)
- most-visited tool (week of 2026-03-07): prayerlock-app.surge.sh (Ramadan timing)

## the limits of this approach

surge.sh free tier doesn't support server-side rendering, custom domains with SSL (you need their paid plan for that, $30/year), or websockets. for static sites, none of those things are problems.

the tools are also not monetizing directly right now. free tools with no login mean zero email capture by default. I'm solving this by adding a voluntary "get notified of updates" email field on 8 of the tools. non-intrusive, never a requirement.

the architecture doesn't scale to every type of product. but for audience-building tools and free utilities, it's the most efficient path from idea to live URL that I've found.

build one. `surge .`. move on to the next one.

# PART 2: QUORA ANSWERS

---

## Answer 1

**Target question:** "What are the best free alternatives to Notion for productivity?"

**Target tools:** habitforge-app.surge.sh, content calendar context (PRINTMAXX use case)

---

Depends on what you're actually using Notion for. Most people use about 15% of what Notion offers and pay $16/month for the privilege.

Here's a breakdown by use case:

**If you're using Notion as a task manager:**
Todoist's free tier is better for pure task management. It has natural language date parsing ("next Tuesday at 3pm"), priority levels, and a working mobile app. Notion's task management is technically capable but not optimized for it.

**If you're using Notion as a wiki or knowledge base:**
Obsidian is free for personal use, stores everything as local Markdown files (so you own your data), and has a better linking system than Notion. The tradeoff: no web access, no collaboration. If you work solo and don't need cloud sync, Obsidian is stronger for knowledge management.

**If you're using Notion as a habit tracker:**
Notion is a bad habit tracker. The database views are flexible but you're maintaining the system manually every day. habitforge-app.surge.sh does one thing: tracks streaks for multiple habits and shows you which ones you've been consistent with. No setup beyond naming your habits. Works offline (it's a PWA). Installs from your browser in 10 seconds.

**If you're using Notion as a content calendar:**
Google Sheets with a shared calendar view handles most content calendar needs for free and with better collaboration than Notion's free tier (which doesn't support real-time collab for multiple users). For scheduling posts on actual social platforms, Buffer's free tier handles 3 channels with 10 queued posts each.

**If you're using Notion as a personal dashboard:**
Start.me (free tier available) or a custom HTML start page hosted on Surge does this without a database system you have to maintain.

The honest answer: Notion is good at being Notion. The free alternatives are usually better at doing one specific thing. Figure out which of those things you actually use, and replace each one with a tool that does it well.

Most people who switch from Notion end up using 3-4 simpler tools instead of one complex one. That's not a downgrade. That's matching the tool to the actual job.

---

## Answer 2

**Target question:** "How do I build a PWA from scratch?"

**Target tools:** scripture-streak-app.surge.sh (as working example), general PWA architecture

---

A PWA needs 3 things: HTTPS, a manifest.json, and a service worker. Everything else is your regular web app.

Here's how to build a working one from scratch. I'll use a streak tracker as the example because the core logic is simple enough to show completely.

**Step 1: the base HTML**

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <meta name="theme-color" content="#2D5016">
  <link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.json">
  <title>My Streak App</title>
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">
</head>
<body>
  <div id="app">
    <h1>Day <span id="streak-count">0</span></h1>
    <p id="status-text">Have you done it today?</p>
    <button id="check-in-btn">Mark complete</button>
    <p id="last-checkin"></p>
  </div>
  <script src="app.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
```

**Step 2: manifest.json**

This is what tells the browser your app is installable.

```json
{
  "name": "My Streak App",
  "short_name": "StreakApp",
  "start_url": "/",
  "display": "standalone",
  "background_color": "#ffffff",
  "theme_color": "#2D5016",
  "icons": [
    { "src": "/icons/icon-192.png", "sizes": "192x192", "type": "image/png" },
    { "src": "/icons/icon-512.png", "sizes": "512x512", "type": "image/png" }
  ]
}
```

`"display": "standalone"` removes the browser chrome. when installed, it looks like a native app.

**Step 3: service worker (sw.js)**

The service worker is what enables offline use and makes the install prompt appear.

```javascript
const CACHE = 'app-v1';
const ASSETS = ['/', '/index.html', '/style.css', '/app.js', '/manifest.json'];

self.addEventListener('install', e => {
  e.waitUntil(caches.open(CACHE).then(c => c.addAll(ASSETS)));
});

self.addEventListener('fetch', e => {
  e.respondWith(
    caches.match(e.request).then(cached => cached || fetch(e.request))
  );
});
```

Register it in your app.js:

```javascript
if ('serviceWorker' in navigator) {
  navigator.serviceWorker.register('/sw.js');
}
```

**Step 4: the streak logic**

```javascript
function getStreak() {
  return JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('streak')) || { count: 0, lastDate: null };
}

function checkIn() {
  const streak = getStreak();
  const today = new Date().toDateString();

  if (streak.lastDate === today) {
    alert('Already checked in today.');
    return;
  }

  const yesterday = new Date(Date.now() - 86400000).toDateString();
  streak.count = streak.lastDate === yesterday ? streak.count + 1 : 1;
  streak.lastDate = today;
  localStorage.setItem('streak', JSON.stringify(streak));
  renderStreak(streak);
}

function renderStreak(streak) {
  document.getElementById('streak-count').textContent = streak.count;
  document.getElementById('last-checkin').textContent =
    streak.lastDate ? `Last: ${streak.lastDate}` : '';
}

document.getElementById('check-in-btn').addEventListener('click', checkIn);
renderStreak(getStreak()); // load on start
```

**Step 5: deploy somewhere with HTTPS**

`surge .` from your project folder gives you a free `.surge.sh` subdomain with HTTPS. That's the third requirement for installability satisfied.

After deploying, open the URL on your phone in Chrome or Safari. On Android, Chrome will prompt you to install. On iOS/Safari, tap the Share button and select "Add to Home Screen."

You now have an installable app with offline support and no App Store, no backend, and no account system. A working example using this exact pattern: scripture-streak-app.surge.sh.

The 4 most common mistakes when building PWAs:
- Forgetting to register the service worker (app won't install)
- Using HTTP instead of HTTPS (service workers require HTTPS, surge.sh handles this for you)
- Icon files that don't exist at the paths manifest.json lists (install fails silently)
- Caching only the root path and not all assets, so the app breaks offline

Build the service worker last. Get the app logic working first. Add installability when the core is solid.

---

## Answer 3

**Target question:** "What is the best prayer reminder app for Muslims?"

**Target tool:** prayerlock-app.surge.sh

**Note: TIME-CRITICAL, Ramadan 2026**

---

The mainstream options have problems worth knowing about before you pick one.

Muslim Pro is the most downloaded app in the category. It was caught selling user location data to third parties (this was reported by Vice/Motherboard in 2020). The company said it stopped, but the core business model of many free apps in this space is data monetization. Worth knowing.

Athan Pro and Prayer Now are solid apps with accurate prayer time calculations and good UI. They require accounts and have ads on the free tiers. The paid versions range from $3 to $10/year, which is reasonable if the tradeoff bothers you.

For Ramadan specifically, there's a free web app worth knowing about: prayerlock-app.surge.sh

What it does: tracks prayer times with no account, no ads, no data collection. It's a PWA (progressive web app), meaning you install it from your phone's browser and it sits on your home screen exactly like a native app. It works offline after the first load.

To install on Android: open the URL in Chrome, tap the three dots in the top right, select "Add to Home Screen." Takes 10 seconds.

To install on iPhone: open the URL in Safari, tap the Share button (box with arrow), select "Add to Home Screen." Same 10-second process.

The tradeoff compared to Muslim Pro or Athan Pro: no automatic prayer time calculation based on GPS location (you set your city manually), no Quran audio library, no community features. It's a tracker and reminder, not a full Islamic lifestyle app.

If you want the full feature set and aren't concerned about the data policies, Athan Pro has accurate calculations, a good Quran reader, and an active development team. The paid tier at $3/year is worth it for what you get.

If you want something with no account, no permissions, and no data leaving your device: prayerlock-app.surge.sh works for that use case. Especially useful now with Ramadan active and 22 days of tracking remaining.

The calculation accuracy for prayer times matters most. All the mainstream apps use established algorithms (Muslim World League, ISNA, Karachi method, etc.). Pick the one your local masjid uses. prayerlock-app.surge.sh uses the Muslim World League method by default, which is widely accepted.

---

## Answer 4

**Target question:** "How can I start freelancing as a web developer with no portfolio?"

**Target approach:** local business demo site method (build free demos, cold email)

---

Build spec work for businesses that don't know you exist yet. Cold email them the live demo. This works faster than building a portfolio site and waiting for inbound.

Here's the exact sequence:

**Step 1: pick a local business category**

Lawn care, dentistry, plumbing, HVAC, auto detailing, roofing, childcare. These businesses are local, often have bad websites, and are not served by web developers who target enterprise clients.

Pick one category and stay in it for the first month. Becoming "the person who builds sites for dentists" is more valuable than being "a web developer."

**Step 2: build a demo for a fictional business in that category**

Don't cold email a real business before you have a demo. Build the demo first. It should look like a real business site: name, phone number, services, a map, mobile-first, fast-loading.

Deploy it to surge.sh (`surge . smith-dentistry-demo.surge.sh` gives you a live URL in 30 seconds, no cost). You now have a link you can put in a cold email.

The demo is your proof. "Here's a site I built for a dental office" with a live URL is more convincing than "here's a screenshot of something I made."

**Step 3: find the actual businesses to email**

Google Maps, Yelp, and local business directories give you name, phone, and sometimes email. For email, check their existing site's contact page, their Facebook business page, or their Google Business profile. Many small businesses list the owner's direct email.

You don't need 1,000 prospects. Start with 50 in one city, one category.

**Step 4: the cold email**

Subject line: "New website for [Business Name]"

Body (keep it under 100 words):

"Hi [Name], I built a demo site for a [category] business in [city] and thought you might want to see it: [demo URL]. Fast, mobile-first, no page builder bloat. If you like the direction, I can customize it for [Business Name] in a few days. My rate for a site like this is $500-$800 depending on pages. Worth a look?"

No long introduction. No agency pitch deck. One link, one price, one ask.

**Step 5: close the first one, then raise your price**

The first client at $500 is not your market rate. It's proof of concept. After you close it and the client is happy, you have a real testimonial and a real case study. Raise to $750 for the next one. Then $1,000.

The demo site approach works because you're showing work before asking for money, and you're making it zero-effort for the prospect to evaluate you. They click a link. They see their industry. They imagine their logo there. That's the whole sales pitch.

Most developers skip this because it feels like doing free work. It's not. The demo takes 3-4 hours to build. If it closes 2 clients at $750 each, that's $375/hour for the demo time. Not free.

---

## Answer 5

**Target question:** "What free tools can I use to audit my website's SEO?"

**Target tools:** sitescore-app.surge.sh, pagescorer.surge.sh

---

Here's what's actually worth using, ranked by how much you'll get out of them.

**Google Search Console (free, most important)**

If you install nothing else, install this. It shows you exactly which queries your pages rank for, which pages Google has indexed, which pages have errors, and your Core Web Vitals scores across your actual traffic. It's Google telling you directly what they see on your site. There is no paid tool that gives you better insight into your own site's performance in Google Search.

Setup: go to search.google.com/search-console, add your property, verify ownership. Takes about 10 minutes.

**Google PageSpeed Insights (free, second most useful)**

Paste any URL and get a Lighthouse audit: performance score, SEO score, accessibility score, and specific recommendations with estimated time savings. The mobile score matters more than the desktop score because Google uses mobile-first indexing.

Target: performance score above 70, SEO score above 90. Anything below 70 on performance is losing you real traffic.

**sitescore-app.surge.sh (free, no account)**

This combines the PageSpeed API scores with on-page checks that PageSpeed doesn't run: title tag length (should be 30-60 characters), meta description presence and length (120-160 characters), H1 count (should be exactly 1), images without alt text, canonical tag, Open Graph tags for social sharing, and structured data. It gives you a single score out of 100 and shows pass/fail for each check. Useful when you want a structured checklist rather than raw numbers.

**pagescorer.surge.sh (free, no account)**

Similar to sitescore but with a different scoring weight and UI. Some people find one more intuitive than the other. Both are free and take 15-30 seconds to run.

**Screaming Frog SEO Spider (free up to 500 URLs)**

The free version crawls up to 500 pages and gives you a full on-page audit: missing title tags, duplicate content, broken internal links, redirect chains, missing meta descriptions, page depth. If you have a site with more than 20 pages, you need this. It's a desktop app (Windows/Mac). Download from screamingfrog.co.uk. The free tier is enough for most small sites.

**Ahrefs Webmaster Tools (free, limited)**

Ahrefs offers a free version of their Webmaster Tools if you verify ownership of your site. It shows you your backlink profile and which keywords you rank for. The paid suite is $99/month but the free webmaster version gives you the most important backlink data at no cost. Worth setting up alongside Search Console.

The workflow: run Search Console weekly to catch indexing problems. Run PageSpeed Insights or sitescore-app.surge.sh before launching any new page. Run Screaming Frog monthly or after major site changes. Check Ahrefs Webmaster Tools monthly for new backlinks and ranking shifts.

That's the complete free stack. The paid tools (Ahrefs, SEMrush, Moz) add competitor analysis, keyword difficulty scores, and historical data. Worth paying for when you're doing SEO at scale or tracking competitors. Not necessary to get started.

## Thread: how to read your site's technical SEO without paying for anything

```
Tweet 1:
free SEO audit stack that covers 90% of what paid tools give you.

here's the exact workflow.

Tweet 2:
start with Google Search Console.

this is Google telling you directly:
- which queries your pages rank for
- which pages they've indexed
- which pages have crawl errors
- your Core Web Vitals by device

nothing paid gives you better signal on your own site. install it first.

Tweet 3:
run Google PageSpeed Insights on your most important pages.

look at:
- mobile performance score (target: above 70)
- SEO score (target: above 90)
- LCP time (target: under 2.5 seconds)
- the specific recommendations sorted by estimated impact

fix the top 3 recommendations. rerun. repeat.

Tweet 4:
for a structured pass/fail checklist:

sitescore-app.surge.sh

checks title tag length, meta description, H1 count, image alt text, canonical tag, Open Graph tags, structured data, and viewport meta

single score out of 100. specific flags. no account needed. runs in 20 seconds.

Tweet 5:
for sites with more than 20 pages, run Screaming Frog.

free up to 500 URLs. desktop app.

it finds: broken internal links, duplicate title tags, redirect chains, missing meta descriptions, pages blocked by robots.txt that shouldn't be

most agencies charge $500+ to run this audit manually. the tool does it in 3 minutes.

Tweet 6:
for backlinks, Ahrefs Webmaster Tools has a free tier.

verify your site, get your backlink profile, see which keywords you rank for.

it's a subset of the paid Ahrefs, but the backlink data is the most valuable piece and you get it for free on your own site.

Tweet 7:
the paid tools (Ahrefs $99/mo, SEMrush $130/mo) add:
- competitor keyword gaps
- historical ranking data
- site-wide crawling at scale
- bulk backlink analysis

worth it when you're doing SEO for clients or running competitive campaigns

not worth it when you're just checking your own site monthly

start free. pay when the free stack becomes the bottleneck.
```
